items.subtopic: Numbness, Tingling, and Sensory Loss | POSITIVE AND NEGATIVE SYMPTOMS
items.title: Understanding Positive Sensory Phenomena: Causes and Clinical Implications
items.content: Positive sensory phenomena such as tingling, itch, and altered sensations arise from excessive activity in sensory pathways due to ectopic impulses. These symptoms occur at sites of lowered threshold or heightened excitability along peripheral or central sensory pathways.
keyPoints: Positive phenomena result from trains of impulses generated at sites of lowered threshold or heightened excitability.
Symptoms include tingling, itch, pricking, lancinations, aching, knifelike, twisting, pulling, tightening, burning, searing, electrical, or raw feelings.
These symptoms are often painful but do not necessarily indicate a sensory deficit on examination.
items.reference: Numbness Tingling and Sensory Loss · Numbness, Tingling, and Sensory Loss | POSITIVE AND NEGATIVE SYMPTOMS

items.subtopic: Numbness, Tingling, and Sensory Loss | POSITIVE AND NEGATIVE SYMPTOMS
items.title: Identifying Negative Sensory Phenomena: Clinical Thresholds and Examination
items.content: Negative sensory phenomena represent a loss of sensation characterized by numbness. Clinically significant sensory deficits are usually demonstrable when at least half of the afferent axons innervating a site are lost or functionless.
keyPoints: Negative phenomena indicate diminished or absent feeling, often experienced as numbness.
A sensory deficit is typically demonstrable on clinical examination if at least one-half of the afferent axons are lost or functionless.
Rapid loss results in conspicuous positive and negative symptoms; slow loss may be unnoticed by patients but detectable via nerve conduction studies.
items.reference: Numbness Tingling and Sensory Loss · Numbness, Tingling, and Sensory Loss | POSITIVE AND NEGATIVE SYMPTOMS

items.title: Understanding Paresthesias vs Dysesthesias: Key Sensory Symptom Distinctions
items.content: Paresthesias and dysesthesias are both terms used to describe abnormal sensory experiences. Paresthesias typically refer to non-painful sensations like tingling or 'pins-and-needles' that may occur spontaneously, whereas dysesthesias encompass all types of abnormal sensations, including painful ones, regardless of stimulus presence.
keyPoints: Paresthesias: Tingling, pins-and-needles; non-painful; sometimes spontaneous.
Dysesthesias: All abnormal sensations, including pain; with or without stimulus.
items.reference: Numbness Tingling and Sensory Loss · TERMINOLOGY

items.title: Differentiating Sensory Abnormalities: Hypesthesia to Hyperpathia
items.content: Various terms describe sensory abnormalities detected during examination. Hypesthesia and hypoesthesia indicate reduced cutaneous sensation, while anesthesia signifies a complete absence of skin sensation. Hyperesthesia refers to increased sensitivity or pain in response to touch, allodynia describes painful perception from non-painful stimuli, hyperalgesia is severe pain from mild noxious stimuli, and hyperpathia includes delayed but intense pain responses.
keyPoints: Hypesthesia/Hypoesthesia: Reduced cutaneous sensation (pressure, light touch, temperature).
Anesthesia: Complete absence of skin sensation (including pinprick).
Hyperesthesia: Increased sensitivity or pain to touch.
Allodynia: Pain from non-painful stimuli (e.g., tuning fork vibration).
Hyperalgesia: Severe pain from mild noxious stimulus.
Hyperpathia: Delayed, intense pain; includes hyperesthesia, allodynia, hyperalgesia.
items.reference: Numbness Tingling and Sensory Loss · TERMINOLOGY

items.title: Understanding Sensory Pathways: Spinothalamic vs. Lemniscal Systems
items.content: The spinothalamic pathway, or anterolateral system, transmits pain, temperature, and crude touch sensations via smaller fibers that cross to the opposite side of the spinal cord and ascend through the brainstem to the thalamus. In contrast, the posterior column-medial lemniscal (lemniscal) pathway carries tactile and proprioceptive information using larger fibers that remain ipsilateral until they synapse in the medulla.
keyPoints: Spinothalamic pathway transmits pain, temperature, itch, and crude touch.
Larger fibers of the lemniscal system transmit tactile and position sense.
Fibers in spinothalamic cross to opposite side at spinal cord level; lemniscal fibers decussate in medulla.
items.reference: Numbness Tingling and Sensory Loss · ANATOMY OF SENSATION

items.title: Sensory Pathway Redundancy and Clinical Implications
items.content: The presence of multiple fiber types ascending both ipsilaterally and contralaterally explains why a complete lesion in the posterior columns may not result in significant sensory deficits, highlighting the redundancy and complexity of sensory pathways.
keyPoints: Multiple fibers ascend diffusely in anterolateral quadrants.
Complete posterior column lesions can have minimal sensory impact due to pathway redundancy.
items.reference: Numbness Tingling and Sensory Loss · ANATOMY OF SENSATION

items.subtopic: Clinical Examination of Sensation | PRIMARY SENSATION
items.title: Testing Pain Sensation with a Clean Pin
items.content: The sense of pain is tested using a clean pin, focusing on the pricking or unpleasant quality rather than pressure. Areas of hypalgesia are mapped radially from the most affected site.
keyPoints: Use a clean pin to test pain sensation.
Patient should focus on pricking/unpleasant quality, not just touch.
Map areas of hypalgesia by proceeding radially.
items.reference: Numbness Tingling and Sensory Loss · Clinical Examination of Sensation | PRIMARY SENSATION

items.subtopic: Clinical Examination of Sensation | PRIMARY SENSATION
items.title: Assessing Joint Position for Proprioception
items.content: Joint position testing evaluates proprioception, starting with the distal interphalangeal joint of the great toe and fingers. Errors lead to testing more proximal joints.
keyPoints: Test joint position with eyes closed.
Begin at distal interphalangeal joints (toe/fingers).
Move to more proximal joints if errors occur.
Proximal test: bring index fingers together with arms extended, eyes closed; normal error <1 cm.
items.reference: Numbness Tingling and Sensory Loss · Clinical Examination of Sensation | PRIMARY SENSATION

items.subtopic: Clinical Examination of Sensation | PRIMARY SENSATION
items.title: Evaluating Vibration Sense with a Tuning Fork
items.content: Vibration sense is tested using an oscillating tuning fork at 128 Hz over bony points, starting distally. Compare thresholds between patient and examiner.
keyPoints: Use a 128 Hz tuning fork for vibration testing.
Begin testing distally on bony points (e.g., big toes, ankles, fingers).
If abnormalities are found, test more proximal sites.
Compare vibratory thresholds with the examiner.
items.reference: Numbness Tingling and Sensory Loss · Clinical Examination of Sensation | PRIMARY SENSATION

items.title: Understanding Two-Point Discrimination for Cortical Lesions
items.content: Two-point discrimination is a key test to assess cortical function, particularly in identifying lesions of the parietal cortex or thalamocortical projections. It involves using calipers set at specific distances and applying them simultaneously to a test site.
keyPoints: Abnormalities in two-point discrimination with normal primary sensation suggest a lesion in the parietal cortex or thalamocortical projections.
On the fingertips, a normal individual can distinguish about a 3-mm separation of points.
Always compare results between analogous sites on both sides of the body.
items.reference: Numbness Tingling and Sensory Loss · CORTICAL SENSATION

items.title: Evaluating Stereognosis for Cortical Sensory Assessment
items.content: Stereognosis is a test to evaluate cortical sensory function by assessing the ability to identify common objects through palpation. It helps in identifying lesions affecting parietal cortex or thalamocortical projections.
keyPoints: Assess stereognosis using standard objects like keys, paper clips, and coins.
Patients should distinguish between similar items such as a dime from a penny and a nickel from a quarter without visual aid.
Astereognosis is indicated if the patient cannot identify objects in one hand but can do so in the other.
items.reference: Numbness Tingling and Sensory Loss · CORTICAL SENSATION

items.subtopic: QUANTITATIVE SENSORY TESTING | ELECTRODIAGNOSTIC STUDIES AND NERVE BIOPSY | LOCALIZATION OF SENSORY ABNORMALITIES (part 1)
items.title: Role of Quantitative Sensory Testing in Serial Evaluation
items.content: Quantitative sensory testing is valuable for serial evaluation of cutaneous sensation, particularly useful in clinical trials. It involves threshold testing for touch, vibratory, and thermal sensations.
keyPoints: Effective sensory testing devices are commercially available.
Threshold testing for touch, vibratory, and thermal sensation is the most widely used application.
items.reference: Numbness Tingling and Sensory Loss · QUANTITATIVE SENSORY TESTING | ELECTRODIAGNOSTIC STUDIES AND NERVE BIOPSY | LOCALIZATION OF SENSORY ABNORMALITIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: QUANTITATIVE SENSORY TESTING | ELECTRODIAGNOSTIC STUDIES AND NERVE BIOPSY | LOCALIZATION OF SENSORY ABNORMALITIES (part 1)
items.title: Limitations of Electrodiagnostic Studies and Nerve Biopsy
items.content: While nerve conduction studies and biopsies are crucial for investigating the peripheral nervous system, they do not assess cutaneous receptors or unmyelinated fibers. Skin biopsy is needed to evaluate these structures.
keyPoints: Nerve conduction studies and nerve biopsy do not evaluate cutaneous receptors or unmyelinated fibers.
Skin biopsy evaluates dermal and epidermal structures.
items.reference: Numbness Tingling and Sensory Loss · QUANTITATIVE SENSORY TESTING | ELECTRODIAGNOSTIC STUDIES AND NERVE BIOPSY | LOCALIZATION OF SENSORY ABNORMALITIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: QUANTITATIVE SENSORY TESTING | ELECTRODIAGNOSTIC STUDIES AND NERVE BIOPSY | LOCALIZATION OF SENSORY ABNORMALITIES (part 1)
items.title: Characteristics of Polyneuropathies
items.content: Polyneuropathies typically present with graded, distal, and symmetric sensory deficits. They often follow a 'stocking glove' distribution, starting in the toes and ascending symmetrically.
keyPoints: Sensory deficits are generally graded, distal, and symmetric.
Dysesthesias begin in the toes and ascend symmetrically.
'Stocking glove' distribution is common.
items.reference: Numbness Tingling and Sensory Loss · QUANTITATIVE SENSORY TESTING | ELECTRODIAGNOSTIC STUDIES AND NERVE BIOPSY | LOCALIZATION OF SENSORY ABNORMALITIES (part 1)

items.title: Dissociated Sensory Loss and its Localization
items.content: A dissociated sensory loss, characterized by impairment of pinprick and temperature appreciation but preservation of light touch, position sense, and vibration, suggests spinothalamic tract involvement. Unilateral deficits with an upper level on the torso indicate spinal cord involvement, while bilateral involvement points to central spinal lesions like syringomyelia.
keyPoints: Dissociated sensory loss involves impairment of pinprick and temperature appreciation.
Preservation of light touch, position sense, and vibration is noted.
Unilateral deficits suggest spinothalamic tract involvement in the spinal cord.
Bilateral involvement indicates central spinal lesions such as syringomyelia.
items.reference: Numbness Tingling and Sensory Loss · LOCALIZATION OF SENSORY ABNORMALITIES (part 2) | TREATMENT

items.title: Lhermitte's Sign and its Implications
items.content: Lhermitte's sign, characterized by an electric shock-like sensation radiating down the back and into the legs upon neck flexion, localizes to cervical lesions affecting posterior columns. Conditions such as multiple sclerosis, cervical spondylosis, or post-irradiation changes can cause this symptom.
keyPoints: Lhermitte's sign involves an electric shock-like sensation radiating from the neck down the back and legs.
It localizes to cervical lesions affecting posterior columns.
Associated conditions include multiple sclerosis, cervical spondylosis, or post-irradiation changes.
items.reference: Numbness Tingling and Sensory Loss · LOCALIZATION OF SENSORY ABNORMALITIES (part 2) | TREATMENT

items.title: Management of Dysesthesias and Thalamic Pain
items.content: Dysesthesias, when severe and persistent, may be managed with anticonvulsants or antidepressants. Thalamic pain, also known as Déjerine-Roussy syndrome, is characterized by severe unilateral pain following thalamic lesions.
keyPoints: Anticonvulsants for dysesthesias: carbamazepine (100–1000 mg/d), gabapentin (300–3600 mg/d), pregabalin (50–300 mg/d).
Antidepressants for dysesthesias: amitriptyline (25–150 mg/d), nortriptyline (25–150 mg/d), desipramine (100–300 mg/d), venlafaxine (75–225 mg/d).
Thalamic pain, or Déjerine-Roussy syndrome, involves severe unilateral pain post-thalamic lesion.
items.reference: Numbness Tingling and Sensory Loss · LOCALIZATION OF SENSORY ABNORMALITIES (part 2) | TREATMENT

items.subtopic: Numbness, Tingling, and Sensory Loss | POSITIVE AND NEGATIVE SYMPTOMS
items.question: Which type of abnormal sensory symptom is characterized by excessive activity in sensory pathways without necessarily being associated with a sensory deficit on examination?
options: Positive phenomena such as tingling or lancinations
Both positive and negative phenomena equally
Neither, as all symptoms are associated with deficits
items.explanation: Positive phenomena represent excessive activity in sensory pathways without necessarily being associated with a sensory deficit.
items.reference: Numbness Tingling and Sensory Loss · Numbness, Tingling, and Sensory Loss | POSITIVE AND NEGATIVE SYMPTOMS

items.subtopic: Numbness, Tingling, and Sensory Loss | POSITIVE AND NEGATIVE SYMPTOMS
items.question: What is the minimum proportion of afferent axons that must be lost or functionless before a sensory deficit can be demonstrated by clinical examination in disorders affecting peripheral sensation?
items.explanation: In disorders affecting peripheral sensation, at least one-half of the afferent axons must be lost or functionless before a sensory deficit can be demonstrated.
items.reference: Numbness Tingling and Sensory Loss · Numbness, Tingling, and Sensory Loss | POSITIVE AND NEGATIVE SYMPTOMS

items.subtopic: Numbness, Tingling, and Sensory Loss | POSITIVE AND NEGATIVE SYMPTOMS
items.question: Which type of phenomena are always measured by sensory signs on examination?
options: Both positive and negative phenomena equally
Neither, as sensory signs do not measure any specific phenomenon
items.explanation: Sensory signs on examination are always a measure of negative phenomena.
items.reference: Numbness Tingling and Sensory Loss · Numbness, Tingling, and Sensory Loss | POSITIVE AND NEGATIVE SYMPTOMS

items.question: Which term describes a complete absence of skin sensation to stimuli including pinprick?
items.explanation: Anesthesia refers to the complete absence of skin sensation, including pinprick.
items.reference: Numbness Tingling and Sensory Loss · TERMINOLOGY

items.question: What term is used for a painful response to a nonpainful stimulus?
items.explanation: Allodynia describes the situation where a nonpainful stimulus is perceived as painful.
items.reference: Numbness Tingling and Sensory Loss · TERMINOLOGY

items.question: Which term refers to severe pain in response to a mildly noxious stimulus?
items.explanation: Hyperalgesia denotes severe pain in response to a mildly noxious stimulus.
items.reference: Numbness Tingling and Sensory Loss · TERMINOLOGY

items.question: Which type of receptor is primarily responsible for responding to tissue-damaging stimuli?
items.explanation: Nociceptors are naked nerve endings that respond optimally to tissue-damaging stimuli.
items.reference: Numbness Tingling and Sensory Loss · ANATOMY OF SENSATION

items.question: Through which pathway do larger fibers, responsible for tactile and position sense, primarily ascend?
items.explanation: The lemniscal pathway (posterior column-medial lemniscal pathway) is responsible for tactile and position sense.
items.reference: Numbness Tingling and Sensory Loss · ANATOMY OF SENSATION

items.question: Where do the cell bodies of first-order neurons conveying somatosensory information from the limbs and trunk reside?
items.explanation: The cell bodies of first-order neurons are located in the dorsal root ganglia (DRG).
items.reference: Numbness Tingling and Sensory Loss · ANATOMY OF SENSATION

items.subtopic: Clinical Examination of Sensation | PRIMARY SENSATION
items.question: Which method is recommended to test pain sensation during a sensory examination?
options: Using a tuning fork at room temperature.
Applying pressure with a wisp of cotton.
Pricking the skin with a clean pin and asking the patient to focus on the pricking quality.
Touching the skin with small containers filled with water.
items.explanation: Pain sensation is tested using a clean pin, focusing on the pricking or unpleasant quality of the stimulus.
items.reference: Numbness Tingling and Sensory Loss · Clinical Examination of Sensation | PRIMARY SENSATION

items.subtopic: Clinical Examination of Sensation | PRIMARY SENSATION
items.question: What is the best way to test temperature sensation in patients?
options: Touching with small containers filled with water at desired temperatures or using metal objects for cold and warm sensations.
items.explanation: Temperature sensation is tested with small containers of hot/cold water or metal objects for different temperature sensations.
items.reference: Numbness Tingling and Sensory Loss · Clinical Examination of Sensation | PRIMARY SENSATION

items.subtopic: Clinical Examination of Sensation | PRIMARY SENSATION
items.question: Where should vibration testing begin during a sensory examination?
options: At bony points, starting distally in the feet and hands.
Using small containers filled with water.
items.explanation: Vibration testing should begin over bony points, starting distally in the feet and hands.
items.reference: Numbness Tingling and Sensory Loss · Clinical Examination of Sensation | PRIMARY SENSATION

items.subtopic: Clinical Examination of Sensation | PRIMARY SENSATION
items.question: How is joint position sense tested for proprioception?
options: By asking the patient to close their eyes while touching a tuning fork.
With passive movement of distal interphalangeal joints and stabilization of proximal joints.
Using small containers filled with water at different temperatures.
Pricking the skin with a clean pin.
items.explanation: Joint position sense is tested by moving distal interphalangeal joints passively while stabilizing more proximal joints, with eyes closed.
items.reference: Numbness Tingling and Sensory Loss · Clinical Examination of Sensation | PRIMARY SENSATION

items.question: Which test involves recognizing letters or numbers drawn on the palm with eyes closed?
items.explanation: Graphesthesia is the ability to recognize letters or numbers drawn on the palm with eyes closed.
items.reference: Numbness Tingling and Sensory Loss · CORTICAL SENSATION

items.question: What does a normal individual typically distinguish as the minimum separation of points in two-point discrimination on the fingertips?
items.explanation: A normal individual can distinguish about a 3-mm separation of points on the fingertips.
items.reference: Numbness Tingling and Sensory Loss · CORTICAL SENSATION

items.question: Which sensory test involves identifying common objects by palpation?
items.explanation: Stereognosis is the ability to identify common objects by palpation.
items.reference: Numbness Tingling and Sensory Loss · CORTICAL SENSATION

items.subtopic: QUANTITATIVE SENSORY TESTING | ELECTRODIAGNOSTIC STUDIES AND NERVE BIOPSY | LOCALIZATION OF SENSORY ABNORMALITIES (part 1)
items.question: Which sensory testing method is particularly useful for serial evaluation of cutaneous sensation in clinical trials?
items.explanation: Quantitative sensory testing is particularly useful for serial evaluation of cutaneous sensation in clinical trials.
items.reference: Numbness Tingling and Sensory Loss · QUANTITATIVE SENSORY TESTING | ELECTRODIAGNOSTIC STUDIES AND NERVE BIOPSY | LOCALIZATION OF SENSORY ABNORMALITIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: QUANTITATIVE SENSORY TESTING | ELECTRODIAGNOSTIC STUDIES AND NERVE BIOPSY | LOCALIZATION OF SENSORY ABNORMALITIES (part 1)
items.question: What is not evaluated by nerve conduction studies and nerve biopsy?
options: Structure of myelinated nerve fibers in the nerve trunks
Function or structure of unmyelinated nerve fibers
items.explanation: Nerve conduction studies and nerve biopsy do not evaluate the function or structure of cutaneous receptors, free nerve endings, or unmyelinated or thinly myelinated nerve fibers.
items.reference: Numbness Tingling and Sensory Loss · QUANTITATIVE SENSORY TESTING | ELECTRODIAGNOSTIC STUDIES AND NERVE BIOPSY | LOCALIZATION OF SENSORY ABNORMALITIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: QUANTITATIVE SENSORY TESTING | ELECTRODIAGNOSTIC STUDIES AND NERVE BIOPSY | LOCALIZATION OF SENSORY ABNORMALITIES (part 1)
items.question: What is a common manifestation of compression of the L5 or S1 root?
items.explanation: Compression of the L5 or S1 root, such as from a ruptured intervertebral disk, commonly manifests as sciatica.
items.reference: Numbness Tingling and Sensory Loss · QUANTITATIVE SENSORY TESTING | ELECTRODIAGNOSTIC STUDIES AND NERVE BIOPSY | LOCALIZATION OF SENSORY ABNORMALITIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: QUANTITATIVE SENSORY TESTING | ELECTRODIAGNOSTIC STUDIES AND NERVE BIOPSY | LOCALIZATION OF SENSORY ABNORMALITIES (part 1)
items.question: Which type of polyneuropathy is characterized by burning, painful dysesthesias with reduced pinprick and thermal sensation but sparing of proprioception?
options: Polyneuropathy due to spinal cord lesions
items.explanation: Small-fiber polyneuropathies are characterized by burning, painful dysesthesias with reduced pinprick and thermal sensation but sparing of proprioception.
items.reference: Numbness Tingling and Sensory Loss · QUANTITATIVE SENSORY TESTING | ELECTRODIAGNOSTIC STUDIES AND NERVE BIOPSY | LOCALIZATION OF SENSORY ABNORMALITIES (part 1)

items.question: Which condition is characterized by severe, persistent unilateral pain following a lesion in the thalamus?
items.explanation: Déjerine-Roussy syndrome involves severe, persistent unilateral pain due to a thalamic lesion.
items.reference: Numbness Tingling and Sensory Loss · LOCALIZATION OF SENSORY ABNORMALITIES (part 2) | TREATMENT

items.question: What is the typical presentation of focal sensory seizures?
options: Unilateral tingling that may spread in a Jacksonian march
Painful sensations confined to one limb
items.explanation: Focal sensory seizures typically present as unilateral tingling with possible spreading in a Jacksonian march.
items.reference: Numbness Tingling and Sensory Loss · LOCALIZATION OF SENSORY ABNORMALITIES (part 2) | TREATMENT

items.question: What symptom is commonly associated with lesions affecting the posterior columns of the cervical spinal cord?
items.explanation: Lhermitte's sign is associated with cervical lesions affecting the posterior columns.
items.reference: Numbness Tingling and Sensory Loss · LOCALIZATION OF SENSORY ABNORMALITIES (part 2) | TREATMENT

items.question: Which medication is NOT typically used for treating dysesthesias?
items.explanation: Ibuprofen is not typically used for treating dysesthesias; anticonvulsants and antidepressants are.
items.reference: Numbness Tingling and Sensory Loss · LOCALIZATION OF SENSORY ABNORMALITIES (part 2) | TREATMENT

items.subtopic: Numbness, Tingling, and Sensory Loss | POSITIVE AND NEGATIVE SYMPTOMS
items.statement: Positive sensory symptoms often result from trains of impulses generated at sites of heightened excitability along a sensory pathway.
items.explanation: Positive phenomena usually result from ectopic impulses due to lowered threshold or heightened excitability.
items.reference: Numbness Tingling and Sensory Loss · Numbness, Tingling, and Sensory Loss | POSITIVE AND NEGATIVE SYMPTOMS

items.subtopic: Numbness, Tingling, and Sensory Loss | POSITIVE AND NEGATIVE SYMPTOMS
items.statement: A rapid rate of sensory fiber loss typically results in only negative phenomena being conspicuous.
items.explanation: A rapid rate of sensory fiber loss usually makes both positive and negative phenomena conspicuous.
items.reference: Numbness Tingling and Sensory Loss · Numbness, Tingling, and Sensory Loss | POSITIVE AND NEGATIVE SYMPTOMS

items.explanation: Paresthesias refer to tingling or pins-and-needles but exclude pain.
items.reference: Numbness Tingling and Sensory Loss · TERMINOLOGY

items.statement: The Romberg sign is positive when a patient sways markedly with eyes closed.
items.explanation: A positive Romberg sign indicates marked swaying or toppling with eyes closed.
items.reference: Numbness Tingling and Sensory Loss · TERMINOLOGY

items.statement: Nociception, itch, temperature sensibility, and touch are primarily subserved by larger myelinated fibers.
items.explanation: These sensations are mainly subserved by smaller fibers (unmyelinated and small myelinated).
items.reference: Numbness Tingling and Sensory Loss · ANATOMY OF SENSATION

items.statement: A complete lesion of the posterior columns may result in significant sensory deficits.
items.explanation: Due to diffusely distributed fibers, a complete lesion of the posterior columns may be associated with little sensory deficit.
items.reference: Numbness Tingling and Sensory Loss · ANATOMY OF SENSATION

items.subtopic: Clinical Examination of Sensation | PRIMARY SENSATION
items.statement: Touch should be tested on hairy skin to ensure accurate sensory examination.
items.explanation: It is better to avoid testing touch on hairy skin due to the profusion of sensory endings around each hair follicle.
items.reference: Numbness Tingling and Sensory Loss · Clinical Examination of Sensation | PRIMARY SENSATION

items.subtopic: Clinical Examination of Sensation | PRIMARY SENSATION
items.statement: Vibratory thresholds can be compared between a patient and an examiner for control purposes.
items.explanation: Comparing vibratory thresholds at the same site in both the patient and examiner is used for control.
items.reference: Numbness Tingling and Sensory Loss · Clinical Examination of Sensation | PRIMARY SENSATION

items.statement: Abnormalities in cortical sensory tests with normal primary sensation indicate a lesion of the parietal cortex or thalamocortical projections.
items.explanation: Such abnormalities signify a lesion of the parietal cortex or thalamocortical projections.
items.reference: Numbness Tingling and Sensory Loss · CORTICAL SENSATION

items.statement: In stereognosis, patients should use both hands simultaneously to identify objects.
items.explanation: Patients should feel the object with only one hand at a time in stereognosis.
items.reference: Numbness Tingling and Sensory Loss · CORTICAL SENSATION

items.subtopic: QUANTITATIVE SENSORY TESTING | ELECTRODIAGNOSTIC STUDIES AND NERVE BIOPSY | LOCALIZATION OF SENSORY ABNORMALITIES (part 1)
items.statement: Dysesthesias restricted to the fifth digit and adjacent one-half of the fourth finger reliably point to a disorder of the ulnar nerve.
items.explanation: Such dysesthesias denote a lesion at the site of the ulnar nerve, commonly at the elbow.
items.reference: Numbness Tingling and Sensory Loss · QUANTITATIVE SENSORY TESTING | ELECTRODIAGNOSTIC STUDIES AND NERVE BIOPSY | LOCALIZATION OF SENSORY ABNORMALITIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: QUANTITATIVE SENSORY TESTING | ELECTRODIAGNOSTIC STUDIES AND NERVE BIOPSY | LOCALIZATION OF SENSORY ABNORMALITIES (part 1)
items.statement: In polyneuropathies, sensory deficits are generally asymmetric and proximal.
items.explanation: Polyneuropathies typically present with graded, distal, and symmetric sensory deficits.
items.reference: Numbness Tingling and Sensory Loss · QUANTITATIVE SENSORY TESTING | ELECTRODIAGNOSTIC STUDIES AND NERVE BIOPSY | LOCALIZATION OF SENSORY ABNORMALITIES (part 1)

items.statement: Syringomyelia typically causes bilateral spinothalamic tract involvement.
items.explanation: Syringomyelia involves the center of the spinal cord, leading to bilateral spinothalamic tract involvement.
items.reference: Numbness Tingling and Sensory Loss · LOCALIZATION OF SENSORY ABNORMALITIES (part 2) | TREATMENT

items.statement: Déjerine-Roussy syndrome is characterized by a loss of position sense.
items.explanation: Déjerine-Roussy syndrome involves severe, persistent pain rather than a loss of position sense.
items.reference: Numbness Tingling and Sensory Loss · LOCALIZATION OF SENSORY ABNORMALITIES (part 2) | TREATMENT

items.subtopic: Clinical Examination of Sensation | PRIMARY SENSATION
items.assertion: In patients with sensory complaints, testing should begin in the center of the affected region.
items.reason: This approach helps define and compare the distribution of any abnormality to root and peripheral nerve territories.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why testing begins at the center.
items.reference: Numbness Tingling and Sensory Loss · Clinical Examination of Sensation | PRIMARY SENSATION

items.subtopic: Clinical Examination of Sensation | PRIMARY SENSATION
items.assertion: The examiner should consider psychological causes in patients with inconsistent sensory examination results.
items.reason: Psychological factors can cause sensory symptoms that do not fit anatomic localization.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why psychological causes should be considered.
items.reference: Numbness Tingling and Sensory Loss · Clinical Examination of Sensation | PRIMARY SENSATION

items.subtopic: QUANTITATIVE SENSORY TESTING | ELECTRODIAGNOSTIC STUDIES AND NERVE BIOPSY | LOCALIZATION OF SENSORY ABNORMALITIES (part 1)
items.assertion: Sensory neuronopathy is characterized by widespread but asymmetric sensory loss.
items.reason: This condition occurs in a non-length-dependent manner and may be paraneoplastic or related to autoimmune diseases.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why sensory neuronopathy presents with widespread but asymmetric sensory loss.
items.reference: Numbness Tingling and Sensory Loss · QUANTITATIVE SENSORY TESTING | ELECTRODIAGNOSTIC STUDIES AND NERVE BIOPSY | LOCALIZATION OF SENSORY ABNORMALITIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: QUANTITATIVE SENSORY TESTING | ELECTRODIAGNOSTIC STUDIES AND NERVE BIOPSY | LOCALIZATION OF SENSORY ABNORMALITIES (part 1)
items.assertion: With a lesion affecting a single root, sensory deficits are always prominent.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because with a single root lesion, sensory deficits may be minimal or absent due to extensive overlap of adjacent root territories.
items.reference: Numbness Tingling and Sensory Loss · QUANTITATIVE SENSORY TESTING | ELECTRODIAGNOSTIC STUDIES AND NERVE BIOPSY | LOCALIZATION OF SENSORY ABNORMALITIES (part 1)

items.assertion: Lhermitte's sign is indicative of cervical lesions affecting the posterior columns.
items.reason: Flexion of the neck can cause an electric shock-like sensation radiating down the back and into the legs in such cases.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Numbness Tingling and Sensory Loss · LOCALIZATION OF SENSORY ABNORMALITIES (part 2) | TREATMENT

items.assertion: Psychogenic sensory symptoms can be diagnosed by their unusual nature.
items.reason: Sensory complaints are considered psychogenic if they have an anatomically difficult-to-explain boundary.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason is not a correct explanation of the assertion.
